deployment_execution_phase:
This phase handles the final deployment of the REST connector(s) after all DSL configuration files have been created. It provides four deployment options: Local (Docker), Serverless, Self-hosted Server, and OpenShift.
<<<<<<< HEAD
Workflow Control and Error Handling
CRITICAL: The mode must follow these workflow control rules throughout all deployment phases:
Wait for User Confirmation After Each Step
After executing ANY command or providing instructions (whether automatic or manual), you MUST wait for the user to confirm success before proceeding.
DO NOT assume success. DO NOT automatically proceed to the next step. ALWAYS wait for explicit user confirmation.
Handle Failures Appropriately
If a step fails or the user reports an error:
1. DO NOT display "Task complete" or "Success" messages 2. DO display: - Clear description of what failed - Error details from the command output (if available) - Specific troubleshooting steps from the error_handling section - Options to retry or get manual instructions
3. Ask the user if they want to: - Retry the failed step - Switch to manual instructions - Skip this deployment option and try another - Get help with troubleshooting
Ask Before Proceeding to Next Phase
After SUCCESSFUL completion of each major phase, explicitly ask the user if they want to proceed to the next phase:
- After Deployment → Ask: "Would you like to proceed with CP4D registration?" - After Registration → Ask: "Would you like to validate the registration with discovery tests?"
DO NOT automatically proceed. Wait for user's explicit choice.
Present clear options: 1. Yes - Proceed to [next phase] 2. No - Stop here (I'll do it later) 3. Help - I need assistance with [current phase]
Success Messages Only on Actual Success
Display "Deployment Successful!" or "Task complete" messages ONLY when: - User explicitly confirms the step succeeded - All verification checks pass - No errors were reported
If there's ANY uncertainty, ask for confirmation instead of assuming success.
Provide Context at Each Step
When asking for confirmation or presenting options, always provide context: - What was just completed - What the next step would be - Why the next step is important - What happens if they choose to skip
======= >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Multiple Connectors Check
After completing Phase 3 (Presentation & Confirmation) for a connector, ask if the user wants to create additional connectors.
Ask About Additional Connectors After successfully creating and confirming a connector configuration
Would you like to create another REST API connector configuration?
Yes, create another connector Return to Phase 1 (API Discovery & Analysis) to create another connector All connectors will be stored in the same resources directory
No, proceed to deployment Continue to Phase 5 (Deployment Option Selection)
Present options clearly: 1. Yes - Create another connector (returns to Phase 1) 2. No - Proceed to deployment
If user chooses to create another connector, repeat Phases 1-3 for the new connector. Continue asking after each connector until user chooses to proceed to deployment.
Multiple connectors can be packaged together in a single deployment. All DSL files in sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources/ will be included in the deployment.
Deployment Option Selection
Present four deployment options and guide the user through the selected deployment process.
Present Deployment Options
How would you like to deploy your REST API connector(s)?
The deployment options presented depend on the deployment type chosen in Phase 0: - Developer option: Shows all 4 deployment options (Local, Serverless, Self-Hosted, OpenShift) - Codeless option: Shows only 3 deployment options (Serverless, Self-Hosted, OpenShift) Local deployment is not available for codeless option as it requires building custom Docker image.
Options when Developer deployment type was chosen in Phase 0
1 Local Docker Deployment
Build and run Docker container on your local machine. Best for: Testing and manual verification of connectors.
Development, testing, local verification Developer option only
2 Serverless Deployment
Deploy Docker container to a serverless platform. Best for: As-a-service solutions with automatic scaling.
Cloud-native, as-a-service deployments Placeholder - Not yet implemented
3 Self-Hosted Server Deployment
Deploy Docker container to your own server infrastructure. Best for: As-a-service solutions with full control.
Custom infrastructure, as-a-service with control
4 OpenShift Deployment
<<<<<<< HEAD Deploy container to OpenShift platform using Flight Operator. Best for: On-premises enterprise deployments.
Enterprise on-premises solutions ======= Deploy container to OpenShift platform. Best for: On-premises enterprise deployments.
Enterprise on-premises solutions Placeholder - Not yet implemented >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)

1 Serverless Deployment
Deploy pre-built Docker container to a serverless platform. Best for: As-a-service solutions with automatic scaling.
Cloud-native, as-a-service deployments Placeholder - Not yet implemented
2 Self-Hosted Server Deployment
Deploy pre-built Docker container to your own server infrastructure. Best for: As-a-service solutions with full control.
Custom infrastructure, as-a-service with control
3 OpenShift Deployment
Deploy pre-built container to OpenShift platform. Best for: On-premises enterprise deployments.
Enterprise on-premises solutions <<<<<<< HEAD ======= Placeholder - Not yet implemented >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
- For Developer option: Present all four deployment options with clear numbering - For Codeless option: Present only three deployment options (exclude Local Docker) - Renumber options for codeless (1-3 instead of 2-4) - Indicate which options are placeholders (not yet implemented) <<<<<<< HEAD <<<<<<< HEAD - Explain that codeless uses pre-built image ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.1 ======= - Explain that codeless uses pre-built image marek02/connectors-forge:latest >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules) ======= - Explain that codeless uses pre-built image ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0 >>>>>>> dacf80e (custom mode can able to deploy on openshift)
Local Docker Deployment User selects option 1 (Local)
Would you like me to execute the deployment commands, or would you prefer to do it yourself?
Automatic - Execute commands for me I will run all necessary Gradle tasks to build and start the Docker container
Manual - I'll do it myself I will provide you with step-by-step instructions
Automatic Local Deployment User chooses automatic execution
Navigate to sdk-gen cd sdk-gen All Gradle tasks must be run from the sdk-gen directory
Build Project ./gradlew build Compiles the project and runs tests sdk-gen This may take several minutes on first run
Generate Flight Application ./gradlew generateFlightApp Generates the Flight service application with all connectors sdk-gen
Build Docker Image ./gradlew dockerBuild Creates Docker image containing the connector(s) sdk-gen Requires Docker to be installed and running
Start Docker Container ./gradlew dockerStart Starts the Docker container with the connector(s) sdk-gen Container will run in the background
Verify Deployment
Confirm that the container is running successfully. The connector(s) should now be accessible via the Flight service.
Inform User
Local deployment complete!
Your REST API connector(s) are now running in a Docker container. The Flight service is accessible and ready to use.
To stop the container, run: ./gradlew dockerStop (from sdk-gen directory)
Report the error details to the user Suggest checking build logs for specific issues Offer to provide manual instructions instead
Inform user that Docker must be installed and running Provide instructions to start Docker
Manual Local Deployment Instructions User chooses manual execution
To deploy your REST API connector(s) locally using Docker, follow these steps:
1. Navigate to the sdk-gen directory: cd sdk-gen
2. Build the project: ./gradlew build
This compiles the project and runs tests. May take several minutes on first run.
3. Generate the Flight application: ./gradlew generateFlightApp
This generates the Flight service application with all your connectors.
4. Build the Docker image: ./gradlew dockerBuild
This creates a Docker image containing your connector(s). Note: Docker must be installed and running on your machine.
5. Start the Docker container: ./gradlew dockerStart
This starts the container with your connector(s) running.
6. Verify the deployment: The connector(s) should now be accessible via the Flight service.
To stop the container later, run: ./gradlew dockerStop
Let me know once you've completed these steps or if you encounter any issues.
Wait for user to confirm they have completed the deployment steps or report any issues encountered.
Serverless Deployment User selects option 2 (Serverless)
Serverless deployment is not yet implemented.
This is a placeholder for future functionality. When implemented, this option will: - Build the Docker image with your connector(s) - Deploy the image to a serverless platform (e.g., AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Run, Azure Functions) - Configure automatic scaling and endpoint management - Provide the service URL for accessing your connector(s)
For now, please choose one of the other deployment options: - Option 1: Local Docker Deployment (for testing) - Option 3: Self-Hosted Server Deployment (for production) <<<<<<< HEAD - Option 4: Openshift deployment (for production) ======= >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
When implemented, this section will include: - Cloud provider selection - Authentication and credentials configuration - Serverless platform-specific deployment commands - Endpoint URL generation and configuration - Monitoring and logging setup
Self-Hosted Server Deployment User selects Self-Hosted option
The deployment process differs based on the deployment type chosen in Phase 0: <<<<<<< HEAD <<<<<<< HEAD - Codeless: Uses deploy-container.sh script with pre-built image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.1) ======= - Codeless: Uses deploy-container.sh script with pre-built image (marek02/connectors-forge:latest) >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules) ======= - Codeless: Uses deploy-container.sh script with pre-built image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0) >>>>>>> dacf80e (custom mode can able to deploy on openshift) - Developer: Uses ./gradlew dockerPush to build and push custom Docker image
Would you like me to execute the deployment, or would you prefer to do it yourself?
Automatic - Execute for me I will run the deployment commands/script to deploy to your server
Manual - I'll do it myself I will provide you with instructions
Automatic Self-Hosted Deployment (Codeless) User chooses automatic execution AND codeless deployment type
<<<<<<< HEAD
CRITICAL: Deployment execution requires careful monitoring and confirmation. - Execute the deployment script and monitor its progress through all 7 steps - Wait for script completion before asking for confirmation - Ask user to confirm deployment succeeded (container running, all 7 steps completed) - Do NOT show success message unless user confirms deployment worked - If deployment fails at any step, provide troubleshooting based on the specific step and error - Only proceed to registration if deployment is confirmed successful
======= >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Ask for SSH Connection Details
Ask the user for SSH connection details using ask_followup_question. Present these as suggested answers with placeholders for the user to fill in.
Please provide your SSH connection details for the remote Docker server:
SSH Host: [hostname/IP], SSH User: [username], SSH Port: 22 SSH Host: [hostname/IP], SSH User: [username], SSH Port: [custom]
Parse the user's response to extract host, user, and port values.
Ask About SSH Key Setup
After receiving SSH connection details, immediately ask if SSH keys are configured.
Do you have SSH key authentication set up for {ssh-user}@{host}?
Yes, I can connect without a password No, I need help setting up SSH keys
Provide SSH Setup Instructions (if needed) User indicates SSH keys are not set up
Provide personalized SSH setup commands using the connection details from step 1.
SSH Key Authentication Setup
To use the deployment script, you need to set up SSH key authentication. Here are the commands personalized for your server:
For Unix/Linux/macOS: ssh-copy-id {ssh-user}@{host} ssh-copy-id -p {ssh-port} {ssh-user}@{host} Run this command and enter your password when prompted
If ssh-copy-id fails or is not available, please consult your system administrator or refer to online SSH documentation for your specific operating system.
For Windows (PowerShell): type $env:USERPROFILE\.ssh\id_rsa.pub | ssh {ssh-user}@{host} "cat >> .ssh/authorized_keys" type $env:USERPROFILE\.ssh\id_rsa.pub | ssh -p {ssh-port} {ssh-user}@{host} "cat >> .ssh/authorized_keys" Run this command and enter your password when prompted
If this command fails, please consult your system administrator or refer to online SSH documentation for Windows.
Verify SSH Access: ssh {ssh-user}@{host} ssh -p {ssh-port} {ssh-user}@{host} You should be able to log in without entering a password
Have you completed the SSH key setup? Can you now connect without a password?
Yes, SSH keys are now configured No, I encountered issues - provide manual deployment instructions instead
Gather Remaining Deployment Parameters SSH authentication is confirmed to be working
Now ask for the deployment-specific parameters. Host, SSH user, and SSH port were already gathered in step 1.
IMPORTANT: Before asking, list the available connector configuration files from sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources/ and explain which connector each file represents to help non-technical users.
List all .json files in sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources/ For each file, read the "$connector_name" field to identify the connector name Present the list to the user in a clear format showing: filename → connector name Ask which files to deploy along with container port and replace flag
I found the following connector configuration files:
Available connectors: - filename1.json → Connector Name 1 - filename2.json → Connector Name 2
Which configuration files would you like to deploy? Please also specify: - Container port (default: 9090) - Replace existing container? (yes/no, default: no)
Deploy: [connector-name.json], Port: 9090 Deploy: [connector1.json connector2.json], Port: 9090, Replace: yes Deploy all connectors, Port: 9090
Parse the response to extract: - files list (can be specific filenames or "all") - port (default to 9090 if not specified) - replace flag (default to false if not specified)
Execute Deployment Script
./connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-container.sh --host {host} --ssh-user {ssh-user} --files {file1} {file2} ... --port {port} [--ssh-port {ssh-port}] [--replace]
<<<<<<< HEAD .\connectors-forge\codeless\deploy-container.ps1 -SshHost {host} -SshUser {ssh-user} -Files {file1},{file2},... -Port {port} [-SshPort {ssh-port}] [-Replace] ======= .\connectors-forge\codeless\deploy-container.ps1 -Host {host} -SshUser {ssh-user} -Files {file1},{file2},... -Port {port} [-SshPort {ssh-port}] [-Replace] >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Runs the OS-specific deployment script with user-provided parameters. The script handles: <<<<<<< HEAD - Connecting to remote server via SSH (using your SSH keys - no password prompts) - Creating container from pre-built Docker image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.1) - Creating tar archive with configuration files and streaming it via SSH ======= - Connecting to remote server via SSH (using your SSH keys) - Pulling the pre-built Docker image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0) - Creating and uploading tar archive with configuration files - Deploying the container to the target server >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules) - Uploading configuration files to /config/mappings/ in the container - Configuring port mapping (9443 internal → user-specified external) - Starting and verifying the container
Project root <<<<<<< HEAD Script uses SSH to execute Docker commands on remote server
The script performs 7 steps: 1. Validate inputs (SSH connectivity, Docker availability, files, port) 2. Check for existing containers (by name or port conflict) 3. Create container (docker create with port mapping) 4. Upload config files (tar streaming via SSH to docker cp) 5. Start container (docker start) 6. Verify container status (docker inspect) 7. Report success with container details
======= Script uses SSH to deploy to remote server >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Monitor Deployment
Monitor the script output for successful deployment confirmation. <<<<<<< HEAD The script provides 7-step progress updates (changed from 8 steps in previous version). ======= The script provides 7-step progress updates. >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Verify Deployment
Confirm that the connector(s) are accessible on the target server. The script will output the container ID and name upon success.
Inform User
Self-hosted deployment complete!
Your REST API connector(s) have been deployed to your server using the pre-built image. The deployment script has configured the necessary networking and access.
Container details and service endpoint information are shown in the script output.
Report the error details from script output <<<<<<< HEAD Verify SSH key authentication is configured correctly (test with: ssh user@host) ======= Verify SSH key authentication is configured correctly >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules) Check if configuration files exist at specified paths Verify port number is valid (1-65535) and not in use (unless --replace is used) Check network connectivity to target server Verify Docker is installed and user has permissions on remote server Offer to provide manual instructions instead
<<<<<<< HEAD
Verify SSH host and port are correct Check SSH key authentication is set up (should connect without password) Test SSH connection manually: ssh -p {port} {user}@{host} Verify firewall allows SSH connections Check if SSH service is running on remote server
Verify Docker is installed on remote server Check user has permissions to run Docker commands Test Docker access: ssh {user}@{host} "docker version" User may need to be added to docker group on remote server
Verify /config directory exists in the container image Check tar command is available (Windows 10+ or PowerShell 7+) Verify SSH connection is stable during file transfer Check disk space on remote server
======= >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Automatic Self-Hosted Deployment (Developer) User chooses automatic execution AND developer deployment type

CRITICAL: Docker image build and push requires confirmation. - Execute dockerPush task and monitor progress - Wait for task completion before asking for confirmation - Ask user to confirm image was successfully pushed - Do NOT show success message unless user confirms push succeeded - If push fails, provide troubleshooting based on error messages - Remind user they still need to deploy the image to their server - Only proceed to next steps if push is confirmed successful
======= >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Navigate to sdk-gen cd sdk-gen Gradle task must be run from sdk-gen directory
Push Docker Image ./gradlew dockerPush
Builds the Docker image with your custom connector(s) and pushes it to the configured container registry. This task handles: - Building the Docker image with your connector code - Tagging the image appropriately - Pushing to your container registry
sdk-gen Container registry must be configured in gradle.properties or environment variables
Verify Push
Confirm that the image was successfully pushed to the container registry.
<<<<<<< HEAD Confirm Success and Inform User true
Did the dockerPush task complete successfully? Check the output for: - "BUILD SUCCESSFUL" message - Image pushed to registry confirmation - No error messages
✓ Docker image push complete!
Your custom Docker image with REST API connector(s) has been pushed to the container registry.
Next steps: - Deploy the image to your server using your deployment tools - Configure the service endpoint and networking - Verify the connector(s) are accessible
✗ Docker image push failed
Please review the error messages above and check: - Container registry credentials are configured - Docker is running - Network connectivity to container registry - Gradle build completed successfully

Docker image push complete!
Your custom Docker image with REST API connector(s) has been pushed to the container registry.
Next steps: - Deploy the image to your server using your deployment tools - Configure the service endpoint and networking - Verify the connector(s) are accessible
>>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Report the error details from Gradle output Check if container registry credentials are configured Verify Docker is running Check network connectivity to container registry Offer to provide manual instructions instead
Manual Self-Hosted Deployment Instructions (Codeless) User chooses manual execution AND codeless deployment type
SSH Key Authentication Setup (Required)
The deploy-container.sh script uses SSH to connect to your remote Docker server. You MUST set up SSH key authentication before running the deployment script.
Unix/Linux/macOS Setup ssh-copy-id user@remote-host Replace 'user' with your username and 'remote-host' with your server address
If ssh-copy-id fails or is not available, please consult your system administrator or refer to online SSH documentation for your specific operating system.
Windows Setup type $env:USERPROFILE\.ssh\id_rsa.pub | ssh user@remote-host "cat >> .ssh/authorized_keys" Replace 'user' with your username and 'remote-host' with your server address
If this command fails, please consult your system administrator or refer to online SSH documentation for Windows.
Verify SSH Access ssh user@remote-host You should be able to log in without entering a password
To deploy your REST API connector(s) to your self-hosted server using the pre-built image:
IMPORTANT: Before proceeding, ensure SSH key authentication is configured (see SSH Prerequisites above).
REQUIRED PARAMETERS: You need to provide the following information to the deployment script:
1. --host: Remote server hostname or IP address (e.g., remote-server.com or 192.168.1.100) SSH must be accessible on this host.
2. --ssh-user: SSH username for remote server (e.g., ubuntu) User must have Docker permissions on remote server.
3. --files: Space-separated list of connector configuration JSON files Location: sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources/ Example: api1.json api2.json
4. --port: Port to expose on the host machine (1-65535) This will be mapped to the container's internal port 9443. Example: 9090
OPTIONAL PARAMETERS: 5. --ssh-port: SSH port (default: 22) Example: 2222
6. --replace: Add this flag to stop and remove existing container using the same name or port
For Unix/Linux/macOS: 1. Ensure the deployment script has execute permissions: chmod +x connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-container.sh
2. Run the deployment script with required parameters: ./connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-container.sh \ --host remote-server.com \ --ssh-user ubuntu \ --files sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources/api1.json api2.json \ --port 9090 \ [--ssh-port 22] \ [--replace]
For Windows: 1. Run the PowerShell deployment script with required parameters: .\connectors-forge\codeless\deploy-container.ps1 ` <<<<<<< HEAD -SshHost "remote-server.com" ` ======= -Host "remote-server.com" ` >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules) -SshUser "ubuntu" ` -Files "sdk-gen\subprojects\connectors_forge_rest\src\main\resources\api1.json","api2.json" ` -Port 9090 ` [-SshPort 22] ` [-Replace]
<<<<<<< HEAD The script performs 7 steps: 1. Validates inputs - Tests SSH connectivity, Docker availability, file existence, and port validity 2. Checks for existing containers - Detects conflicts by name or port 3. Creates container - Uses docker create with port mapping (pulls image if needed) 4. Uploads config files - Creates tar archive and streams it via SSH to docker cp 5. Starts container - Executes docker start command 6. Verifies status - Checks that container is running 7. Reports success - Displays container ID, name, and port mapping
How it works: - Connects to remote server via SSH using your SSH keys (no password prompts) - Executes Docker commands remotely via SSH - Uses pre-built image: ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.1 - Streams configuration files directly via SSH pipe (no intermediate files on remote server) - Uploads files to /config/mappings/ in the container - Configures port mapping (9443 internal → your specified external port) ======= The script will: - Connect to your remote server via SSH (using your SSH keys) - Pull the pre-built Docker image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0) - Create and upload tar archive with your configuration files - Deploy the container to your target server - Upload configuration files to /config/mappings/ in the container - Configure port mapping (9443 internal → your specified external port) - Start and verify the container >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Prerequisites: - SSH key authentication configured (REQUIRED - see SSH Prerequisites above) - Docker installed on the remote server - User has Docker permissions on remote server - Network connectivity to the target server - Configuration files exist at the specified paths <<<<<<< HEAD - Windows: PowerShell 5.1 or later with tar command support (Windows 10+ or PowerShell 7+) ======= - Windows: PowerShell 5.1 or later >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
The script will output the container ID, name, and deployment status upon successful completion.
Let me know once you've completed the deployment or if you encounter any issues.
Wait for user to confirm they have completed the deployment or report any issues encountered.
Manual Self-Hosted Deployment Instructions (Developer) User chooses manual execution AND developer deployment type
To deploy your custom REST API connector(s) to your self-hosted server:
1. Navigate to the sdk-gen directory: cd sdk-gen
2. Build and push your Docker image to the container registry: ./gradlew dockerPush
This will: - Build a Docker image with your custom connector code - Tag the image appropriately - Push it to your configured container registry
Prerequisites: - Docker installed and running on your machine - Container registry credentials configured in gradle.properties or environment variables - Network connectivity to the container registry
3. After the image is pushed, deploy it to your server using your deployment tools
4. Configure the service endpoint and networking on your server
5. Verify the connector(s) are accessible
Let me know once you've completed the deployment or if you encounter any issues.
Wait for user to confirm they have completed the deployment or report any issues encountered.
<<<<<<< HEAD OpenShift Deployment (Codeless) User selects OpenShift option for codeless deployment
This step is for CODELESS deployment to OpenShift. It uses the pre-built Docker image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.1) from GitHub Container Registry. For DEVELOPER deployment to OpenShift, see step_6_handle_openshift_deployment_developer.
Would you like me to execute the OpenShift deployment commands, or would you prefer to do it yourself?
Automatic - Execute commands for me I will run the OpenShift CLI commands to deploy your connector(s)
Manual - I'll do it myself I will provide you with step-by-step instructions
Automatic OpenShift Deployment User chooses automatic execution
OpenShift CLI (oc) must be installed Deployment properties file must be configured
NEVER ask the user for OpenShift credentials (username, password, or token) directly. ALWAYS show the properties file structure with placeholders and instruct the user to fill it in themselves.
Instruct User to Configure Properties File
Before deploying to OpenShift, you need to configure the deployment properties file.
1. Copy the template file: cp connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.properties.template connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.properties
2. Edit the properties file and fill in your OpenShift cluster details:
File: connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.properties
# OpenShift Cluster Configuration OPENSHIFT_CLUSTER_URL=https://api.cluster.example.com:6443
# Authentication - Choose ONE method: # Method 1: Username/Password OPENSHIFT_USERNAME=your-username OPENSHIFT_PASSWORD=your-password
# Method 2: Token (comment out username/password if using token) # OPENSHIFT_TOKEN=your-token
# Project Configuration OPENSHIFT_PROJECT=your-project-name CREATE_PROJECT_IF_NOT_EXISTS=true RECREATE_CONFIGMAP=true
3. Save the file
Once you've configured the properties file, let me know and I'll proceed with the deployment.
true
Execute Deployment Script
Run the OpenShift deployment script based on your operating system.
chmod +x connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.sh Make the script executable
./connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.sh Execute the deployment script
.\connectors-forge\codeless\deploy-openshift.ps1 Execute the deployment script
Wait for Deployment Completion
The deployment script will: 1. Login to OpenShift cluster 2. Select or create the project/namespace 3. Create ConfigMap from connector configuration files 4. Deploy the connector using deployment.yaml 5. Create the service using service.yaml 6. Verify the deployment status
Wait for the script to complete and report the results.
Properties file not found Instruct user to copy template: cp connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.properties.template connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.properties Instruct user to fill in their OpenShift cluster details
Check the script output for specific error messages Common issues: - OpenShift CLI (oc) not installed - Invalid credentials in properties file - Network connectivity issues - Insufficient permissions in OpenShift cluster - Configuration files not found
Offer to provide manual deployment instructions
Check if configmap already exists (delete and recreate) Verify connector configuration files exist in the specified path
Check pod logs: oc logs -l app=cf-flight-connector Verify image can be pulled from GitHub Container Registry Check resource quotas in the namespace
Manual OpenShift Deployment Instructions User chooses manual execution
NEVER ask the user for OpenShift credentials (username, password, or token) directly. ALWAYS show the properties file structure with placeholders and instruct the user to fill it in themselves.
To deploy your REST API connector(s) to OpenShift, you can use the provided deployment script:
STEP 1: Configure Properties File
1. Copy the template file: cp connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.properties.template connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.properties
2. Edit the properties file and fill in your OpenShift cluster details:
File: connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.properties
# OpenShift Cluster Configuration OPENSHIFT_CLUSTER_URL=https://api.cluster.example.com:6443
# Authentication - Choose ONE method: # Method 1: Username/Password OPENSHIFT_USERNAME=your-username OPENSHIFT_PASSWORD=your-password
# Method 2: Token (comment out username/password if using token) # OPENSHIFT_TOKEN=your-token
# Project Configuration OPENSHIFT_PROJECT=your-project-name CREATE_PROJECT_IF_NOT_EXISTS=true RECREATE_CONFIGMAP=true
3. Save the file
STEP 2: Run Deployment Script
For Unix/Linux/macOS: 1. Make the script executable: chmod +x connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.sh
2. Run the script: ./connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-openshift.sh
For Windows: 1. Run the PowerShell script: .\connectors-forge\codeless\deploy-openshift.ps1
The script will automatically: 1. Login to OpenShift cluster 2. Select or create the project/namespace 3. Create ConfigMap from connector configuration files 4. Deploy the connector using deployment.yaml 5. Create the service using service.yaml 6. Verify the deployment status
ACCESSING THE CONNECTOR: The connector will be accessible within the OpenShift cluster at: cf-flight-connector-service..svc.cluster.local:9443
For external access, you may need to create a Route: oc expose service cf-flight-connector-service
Let me know once you've completed the deployment or if you encounter any issues.
Wait for user to confirm they have completed the deployment or report any issues encountered.
connectors-forge/codeless/deployment.yaml
Defines the Kubernetes Deployment resource: - Image: ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.1 - Namespace: connectorsforge (can be changed) - ConfigMap mount: /config/mappings - Resource limits: 256Mi memory, 200m CPU
connectors-forge/codeless/service.yaml
Defines the Kubernetes Service resource: - Type: ClusterIP - Port: 9443 (gRPC) - Selector: app=cf-flight-connector
OpenShift Deployment (Developer) User selects OpenShift option for developer deployment
This step is for DEVELOPER deployment to OpenShift using the Flight Operator. It builds a custom Docker image with your connector code and deploys it using the FlightService operator. This follows the process documented in guide.md #flight-operator section.
Would you like me to execute the OpenShift deployment commands, or would you prefer to do it yourself?
Automatic - Execute commands for me I will run the build and deployment commands for OpenShift
Manual - I'll do it myself I will provide you with step-by-step instructions
Automatic OpenShift Deployment (Developer) User chooses automatic execution
OpenShift CLI (oc) must be installed User must be logged into OpenShift cluster with appropriate credentials kubectl must be installed make must be installed podman or docker must be installed
Navigate to sdk-gen Directory cd sdk-gen All Gradle tasks must be run from the sdk-gen directory Project root
Apply Code Formatting ./gradlew spotlessApply Applies code formatting rules to ensure consistency sdk-gen
Build Project ./gradlew build Compiles the project and runs tests sdk-gen This may take several minutes on first run
Generate Flight Application ./gradlew generateFlightApp
Generates the Flight service application with all connectors. By default, generates as a web app for WebSphere Liberty container.
sdk-gen
-PstandaloneFlightSvc=true
Use this flag if you prefer to deploy the server as a standalone application instead of a WebSphere Liberty web app.
Build Docker Image ./gradlew dockerBuild Creates Docker image containing the connector(s) sdk-gen Requires Docker or Podman to be installed and running
Navigate to Flight Operator Directory cd ../sdk-operator/flightservice Change to the Flight Operator directory for deployment sdk-gen
Deploy Flight Operator ./gradlew deploy-operator
Deploys the FlightService operator to OpenShift. This creates the flightservice-operator-system namespace and sets up the operator.
sdk-operator/flightservice
Before running, ensure you are logged into OpenShift with appropriate credentials. See guide.md #configure-openshift-login for authentication options.
Push Image to OpenShift Registry cd ../../sdk-gen && ./gradlew dockerPush
Pushes the Docker image to the OpenShift Container Registry. The image will be available in the flightservice-operator-system namespace.
sdk-operator/flightservice
Create FlightService Deployment File
Create a YAML file to deploy your connector as a FlightService. The file should be named after your connector (e.g., rest-connector-flight-service.yaml).
apiVersion: flight.wdp.ibm.com/v1alpha1 kind: FlightService metadata: name: rest-connector spec: replicas: 1 dockerImage: default-route-openshift-image-registry.apps.{your-openshift-domain}/flightservice-operator-system/rest-connector-flight-service:{version}
Replace {your-openshift-domain} with your actual OpenShift domain. Replace {version} with your image version (check gradle.properties or build output).
Deploy FlightService oc apply -f rest-connector-flight-service.yaml Deploys the connector using the FlightService operator Project root
Verify Deployment oc get deployment Check that the deployment is ready
NAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGE rest-connector 1/1 1 1 1m
Verify Pods Running oc get pods Confirm the connector pod is running
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE rest-connector-6fd7c98d8-7dqdr 1/1 Running 0 1m
Inform User
OpenShift deployment complete!
Your REST API connector(s) have been deployed to OpenShift using the Flight Operator.
Deployment details: - Namespace: flightservice-operator-system - FlightService: rest-connector - Replicas: 1
The connector is now running and accessible within the cluster.
Report the error details from Gradle output Check if all dependencies are properly configured Verify Java version compatibility Offer to provide manual instructions instead
Verify OpenShift cluster URL is correct Check username and password are valid Ensure network connectivity to the cluster Refer to guide.md #configure-openshift-login for authentication options
Check if user has appropriate OpenShift permissions Verify TLS certificates are properly configured (see guide.md #save-tls-certificate) Check if required tools (oc, kubectl, make, docker/podman) are installed Review operator logs for specific errors
Verify OpenShift Container Registry is accessible Check if TLS certificates are properly configured Ensure user has permissions to push to the registry Verify image name and tag are correct
Check FlightService YAML syntax is correct Verify image path and version are accurate Check if FlightService operator is running: oc get pods -n flightservice-operator-system Review pod logs: oc logs -l app=rest-connector
Manual OpenShift Deployment Instructions (Developer) User chooses manual execution
To deploy your REST API connector(s) to OpenShift using the Flight Operator:
PREREQUISITES: - OpenShift CLI (oc) installed on your machine - kubectl installed - make installed - podman or docker installed - Access to an OpenShift cluster with appropriate permissions - Logged into OpenShift cluster (oc login)
IMPORTANT: TLS Certificate Setup If your OpenShift cluster uses a TLS certificate not signed by a well-known CA, you must configure it before proceeding. See guide.md #save-tls-certificate for details.
DEPLOYMENT STEPS:
1. Navigate to the sdk-gen directory: cd sdk-gen
2. Apply code formatting: ./gradlew spotlessApply
This ensures code consistency across the project.
3. Build the project: ./gradlew build
This compiles the project and runs tests. May take several minutes on first run.
4. Generate the Flight application: ./gradlew generateFlightApp
OR, for standalone deployment (not WebSphere Liberty): ./gradlew generateFlightApp -PstandaloneFlightSvc=true
This generates the Flight service application with all your connectors.
5. Build the Docker image: ./gradlew dockerBuild
This creates a Docker image containing your connector(s). Requires Docker or Podman to be installed and running.
6. Configure OpenShift authentication (if not already logged in):
Option A: Login via oc command: oc login -u -p
Option B: Configure credentials in gradle.properties: - Edit sdk-operator/flightservice/gradle.properties and add: openshift.console.url=https://console-openshift-console.apps.your-cluster.com:6443 - Add credentials to ~/.gradle/encrypted.gradle.properties: cd sdk-operator/flightservice ./gradlew addCredentials --key "openshift.username" --value ./gradlew addCredentials --key "openshift.password" --value
7. Deploy the FlightService operator: cd ../sdk-operator/flightservice ./gradlew deploy-operator
This deploys the operator to OpenShift and creates the flightservice-operator-system namespace.
8. Push the Docker image to OpenShift Container Registry: cd ../../sdk-gen ./gradlew dockerPush
This pushes your connector image to the OpenShift registry.
9. Create a FlightService deployment file: Create a file named rest-connector-flight-service.yaml with this content:
```yaml apiVersion: flight.wdp.ibm.com/v1alpha1 kind: FlightService metadata: name: rest-connector spec: replicas: 1 dockerImage: default-route-openshift-image-registry.apps.{your-domain}/flightservice-operator-system/rest-connector-flight-service:{version} ```
Replace: - {your-domain} with your OpenShift cluster domain - {version} with your image version (check gradle.properties or build output)
To find your OpenShift registry route: oc get routes -n openshift-image-registry
10. Deploy the FlightService: oc apply -f rest-connector-flight-service.yaml
This creates the deployment using the FlightService operator.
11. Verify the deployment: oc get deployment
You should see: NAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGE rest-connector 1/1 1 1 1m
12. Verify the pod is running: oc get pods
You should see: NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE rest-connector-6fd7c98d8-7dqdr 1/1 Running 0 1m
13. (Optional) View logs if needed: oc logs -l app=rest-connector
ACCESSING THE CONNECTOR: The connector is accessible within the OpenShift cluster. The service endpoint will be available through the FlightService operator.
UPDATING THE DEPLOYMENT: To update your connector: 1. Make code changes 2. Rebuild: ./gradlew build 3. Regenerate Flight app: ./gradlew generateFlightApp 4. Rebuild image: ./gradlew dockerBuild 5. Push updated image: ./gradlew dockerPush 6. Restart deployment: oc rollout restart deployment rest-connector
For more details, refer to guide.md #flight-operator section.
Let me know once you've completed the deployment or if you encounter any issues.
Wait for user to confirm they have completed the deployment or report any issues encountered.
======= OpenShift Deployment User selects option 3 (OpenShift) for codeless deployment
This step is for users who want to deploy their connector(s) to OpenShift. It uses the pre-built Docker image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0) from GitHub Container Registry.
Would you like me to execute the OpenShift deployment commands, or would you prefer to do it yourself?
Automatic - Execute commands for me I will run the OpenShift CLI commands to deploy your connector(s)
Manual - I'll do it myself I will provide you with step-by-step instructions
Automatic OpenShift Deployment User chooses automatic execution
OpenShift CLI (oc) must be installed User must have OpenShift cluster credentials User must have permissions to create resources in the target project
Gather OpenShift Connection Details
Ask the user for OpenShift connection details: 1. OpenShift cluster URI (e.g., https://api.cluster.example.com:6443) 2. Username 3. Password 4. Project/namespace name (where to deploy the connector)
Login to OpenShift oc login -u {username} -p {password} {cluster_uri}
Authenticate with the OpenShift cluster using provided credentials.
Project root
Select Project oc project {project_name}
Switch to the target project/namespace where the connector will be deployed.
Create ConfigMap from Connector Files oc create configmap connectors-config --from-file=sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources
Create a ConfigMap containing all connector configuration JSON files. This ConfigMap will be mounted into the container at /config/mappings.
If configmap already exists, delete it first: oc delete configmap connectors-config
Apply Deployment Configuration oc apply -f connectors-forge/codeless/deployment.yaml
Deploy the connector container using the deployment configuration. This creates a Deployment resource with the connector image and mounts the ConfigMap.
Project root
Apply Service Configuration oc apply -f connectors-forge/codeless/service.yaml
Create a Service to expose the connector within the cluster. The service exposes port 9443 for gRPC communication.
Project root
Verify Deployment oc get pods -l app=cf-flight-connector
Check that the connector pod is running successfully.
Get Service Details oc get service cf-flight-connector-service
Display the service details including the ClusterIP and port.
Inform User
OpenShift deployment complete!
Your REST API connector(s) have been deployed to OpenShift.
Deployment details: - Namespace: {project_name} - Deployment: cf-flight-connector - Service: cf-flight-connector-service - Port: 9443 (gRPC)
The connector is accessible within the cluster at: cf-flight-connector-service.{project_name}.svc.cluster.local:9443
Verify OpenShift cluster URI is correct Check username and password are valid Ensure network connectivity to the cluster
Verify the project/namespace exists Check user has access to the project Offer to create the project if it doesn't exist
Check if configmap already exists (delete and recreate) Verify connector configuration files exist in the specified path
Check pod logs: oc logs -l app=cf-flight-connector Verify image can be pulled from GitHub Container Registry Check resource quotas in the namespace
Manual OpenShift Deployment Instructions User chooses manual execution
To deploy your REST API connector(s) to OpenShift:
PREREQUISITES: - OpenShift CLI (oc) installed on your machine - Access to an OpenShift cluster - Permissions to create resources in your target project/namespace
DEPLOYMENT STEPS:
1. Login to your OpenShift cluster: oc login -u -p
Example: oc login -u developer -p mypassword https://api.cluster.example.com:6443
2. Select the project (namespace) where you want to deploy: oc project
Example: oc project connectorsforge
If the project doesn't exist, create it first: oc new-project connectorsforge
3. Create a ConfigMap with your connector configuration files: oc create configmap connectors-config --from-file=sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources
This command: - Creates a ConfigMap named "connectors-config" - Includes all JSON files from the specified directory - These files will be mounted in the container at /config/mappings
If the ConfigMap already exists, delete it first: oc delete configmap connectors-config
4. Deploy the connector using the deployment configuration: oc apply -f connectors-forge/codeless/deployment.yaml
This creates: - A Deployment with 1 replica - Uses image: ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0 - Mounts the connectors-config ConfigMap - Exposes port 9443 for gRPC
5. Create the service to expose the connector: oc apply -f connectors-forge/codeless/service.yaml
This creates: - A ClusterIP service - Exposes port 9443 - Makes the connector accessible within the cluster
6. Verify the deployment: oc get pods -l app=cf-flight-connector
Wait until the pod status shows "Running"
7. Check the service: oc get service cf-flight-connector-service
Note the ClusterIP and port for accessing the connector
8. View logs (if needed): oc logs -l app=cf-flight-connector
This helps troubleshoot any issues
ACCESSING THE CONNECTOR: The connector is accessible within the OpenShift cluster at: cf-flight-connector-service..svc.cluster.local:9443
For external access, you may need to create a Route: oc expose service cf-flight-connector-service
UPDATING THE DEPLOYMENT: If you need to update connector configurations: 1. Delete the existing ConfigMap: oc delete configmap connectors-config 2. Recreate it with updated files: oc create configmap connectors-config --from-file=sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources 3. Restart the deployment: oc rollout restart deployment cf-flight-connector
Let me know once you've completed the deployment or if you encounter any issues.
Wait for user to confirm they have completed the deployment or report any issues encountered.
connectors-forge/codeless/deployment.yaml
Defines the Kubernetes Deployment resource: - Image: ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0 - Namespace: connectorsforge (can be changed) - ConfigMap mount: /config/mappings - Resource limits: 256Mi memory, 200m CPU
connectors-forge/codeless/service.yaml
Defines the Kubernetes Service resource: - Type: ClusterIP - Port: 9443 (gRPC) - Selector: app=cf-flight-connector
>>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Cloud Pak for Data Registration
After successful deployment, register the connector(s) with Cloud Pak for Data. The registration process differs based on the deployment type chosen in Phase 0.
Ask Registration Preference <<<<<<< HEAD ONLY after successful deployment completion AND user confirmation
IMPORTANT: This step should ONLY be reached if: 1. The deployment step completed successfully 2. User explicitly confirmed the deployment succeeded 3. No errors were reported
If deployment failed or user reported issues, DO NOT ask about registration. Instead, focus on troubleshooting the deployment failure.
Before asking the question, provide context: - Confirm what was just deployed (deployment type, location) - Explain that registration makes the connector available in CP4D - Note that registration can be done later if preferred
Your connector(s) have been successfully deployed.
Would you like to register them with Cloud Pak for Data now?
Registration will: - Make the connector(s) available in your CP4D environment - Allow users to create connections using these connectors - Enable data discovery and access through CP4D ======= After successful deployment completion
Would you like to register your connector(s) with Cloud Pak for Data now? >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Yes, register now Proceed to registration process based on deployment type
No, I'll register later Skip registration and complete the workflow <<<<<<< HEAD User can run registration scripts manually later
Help - I need more information about registration Explain registration process and requirements ======= >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
<<<<<<< HEAD Present options clearly with context: 1. Yes - Register connector(s) with CP4D now 2. No - Skip registration (can be done later manually) 3. Help - Learn more about the registration process
Connector Registration User chooses registration (applies to both developer and codeless deployment types) ======= Present options clearly: 1. Yes - Register connector(s) with CP4D now 2. No - Skip registration (can be done later)
Developer Option Registration User chooses registration AND developer deployment type was selected in Phase 0
Verify Properties File Creation
Before registration, you need to create a properties file with your CP4D environment configuration. This file is not visible to me (it's in .bobignore), so you need to create it manually.
Create Properties File
Create a properties file in the following location: sdk-gen/subprojects/flight/src/dist/resources/payload/envs/
Use a descriptive name like: myenv.properties, production.properties, or dev.properties
Base your file on the template at: sdk-gen/subprojects/flight/src/dist/resources/payload/envs/template.properties
Configure Required Properties
Your properties file must include: - apikey: Your IBM Cloud API key - auth_uri: Authentication endpoint (default: https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token) - datasource_types_uri: Datasource types API endpoint - flight_uri: URI of your deployed Flight service (grpc://host:port) - ssl_certificate_path: Path to your Flight service certificate (optional) - ssl_certificate_validation: Set to true/false
Example configuration: apikey=your_ibm_cloud_api_key_here auth_uri=https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token datasource_types_uri=https://api.dataplatform.cloud.ibm.com:443/v2/datasource_types flight_uri=grpc://your-server:50051 ssl_certificate_validation=false
Have you created the properties file with your CP4D environment configuration? Please provide the name of your properties file (without .properties extension).
Ask Execution Preference After user confirms properties file is created
Would you like me to execute the registration, or would you prefer to do it yourself?
Automatic - Execute registration for me I will run the Gradle register task with your environment configuration
Manual - I'll do it myself I will provide you with instructions to run the registration
Automatic Registration (Developer) User chooses automatic execution
Navigate to sdk-gen cd sdk-gen Gradle task must be run from sdk-gen directory
Run Registration Task ./gradlew register -Penv={env_name}
Registers the connector(s) with Cloud Pak for Data using the specified environment configuration. Replace {env_name} with the properties file name provided by the user.
sdk-gen The env_name should match the properties file name without .properties extension
Verify Registration
Confirm that the registration completed successfully by checking the task output.
Inform User
Registration complete!
Your REST API connector(s) have been registered with Cloud Pak for Data. The connector(s) are now available for use in your CP4D environment.
You can now create connections and access your REST API data through CP4D.
Report the error details from Gradle output Verify the properties file exists and contains correct values Check network connectivity to CP4D endpoints Verify API key has proper permissions Offer to provide manual instructions instead
Manual Registration Instructions (Developer) User chooses manual execution
To register your REST API connector(s) with Cloud Pak for Data:
1. Ensure you have created your properties file in: sdk-gen/subprojects/flight/src/dist/resources/payload/envs/{your_env_name}.properties
2. Navigate to the sdk-gen directory: cd sdk-gen
3. Run the registration task with your environment name: ./gradlew register -Penv={your_env_name}
Replace {your_env_name} with your properties file name (without .properties extension) For example, if your file is myenv.properties, use: -Penv=myenv
The registration task will: - Authenticate with IBM Cloud using your API key - Register the connector datasource type with CP4D - Configure the Flight service endpoint - Make the connector(s) available in your CP4D environment
Prerequisites: - Valid IBM Cloud API key with proper permissions - Network connectivity to CP4D endpoints - Deployed and accessible Flight service
Let me know once you've completed the registration or if you encounter any issues.
Wait for user to confirm they have completed the registration or report any issues encountered.
Codeless Option Registration User chooses registration AND codeless deployment type was selected in Phase 0 >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Create Properties File from Template
Before registration, you need to create a properties file based on the provided template. This file is not visible to me (it's in .bobignore), so you need to create it manually.
<<<<<<< HEAD
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION - NEVER ASK FOR CREDENTIALS DIRECTLY:
You MUST NOT ask the user for any of the following information directly: - API keys - Passwords - Usernames - Tokens - Hostnames - URLs - Any other sensitive or configuration data
Instead, you MUST: 1. Show the file structure with PLACEHOLDERS (e.g., "your-api-key-here", "") 2. Explain what each field means and where to find the values 3. Provide examples with placeholder values 4. Instruct the user to replace placeholders with their actual values 5. Reference the template file for additional guidance
NEVER use phrases like: - "What is your API key?" - "Please provide your password" - "Enter your username" - "What is your hostname?"
ALWAYS use phrases like: - "Replace with your actual API key" - "Fill in your credentials in the properties file" - "Update the placeholder values with your actual configuration"

Review Template File
A template file is provided at: <<<<<<< HEAD connectors-forge/scripts/register-envs.properties.template ======= connectors-forge/codeless/register-envs.properties.template >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
This template shows the required configuration structure with placeholder values.
Create Properties File
Copy the template file to create your configuration:

Configure Required Properties
Edit register-envs.properties and replace all placeholder values (marked with ):
<<<<<<< HEAD AUTHENTICATION (choose ONE method):
Option 1: IBM Cloud API Key (for IBM Cloud deployments) - apikey: Replace with your IBM Cloud API key - Keep username and password commented out
Option 2: Username/Password (for on-premises CP4D deployments) - Comment out the apikey line - Uncomment username and password lines - username: Replace with your CP4D username - password: Replace with your CP4D password
FLIGHT SERVICE (required): - flight_hostname: Replace with your deployed Flight service hostname - flight_port: Replace with your Flight service port number
ENDPOINTS (update for on-premises if needed): - auth_uri: Authentication endpoint * IBM Cloud: https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token (default) * On-premises: https:///icp4d-api/v1/authorize - datasource_types_uri: Datasource types API endpoint * IBM Cloud: https://api.dataplatform.cloud.ibm.com:443/v2/datasource_types (default) * On-premises: https:///v2/datasource_types
OPTIONAL properties: - origin_country: Origin country/region for connector registration (default: us) ======= REQUIRED properties to configure: - apikey: Replace with your IBM Cloud API key - flight_hostname: Replace with your deployed Flight service hostname - flight_port: Replace with your Flight service port number

Ask Execution Preference After user confirms properties file is configured
Would you like me to execute the registration, or would you prefer to do it yourself?
Automatic - Execute registration for me I will run the registration script with your configuration

<<<<<<< HEAD Automatic Registration (Developer) ======= Automatic Registration (Codeless) >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules) User chooses automatic execution
Execute Registration Script <<<<<<< HEAD connectors-forge/scripts/register-connector.sh connectors-forge\scripts\register-connector.ps1
Runs the OS-specific registration script that handles: - Reading configuration from register-envs.properties - Authenticating with IBM Cloud (API key) or on-premises CP4D (username/password) - Obtaining bearer token for API access - Registering the connector with CP4D - Configuring the Flight service endpoint
The script automatically detects which authentication method to use based on which credentials are provided in the properties file. ======= connectors-forge/codeless/register-connector.sh connectors-forge\codeless\register-connector.ps1
Runs the OS-specific registration script that handles: - Reading configuration from register-envs.properties - Authenticating with IBM Cloud - Registering the connector with CP4D - Configuring the Flight service endpoint >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
The script selection is based on the operating system: - Unix/Linux/macOS: register-connector.sh (requires execute permissions: chmod +x) - Windows: register-connector.ps1 (PowerShell script)
Monitor Registration
Monitor the script output for successful registration confirmation.
Verify Registration
Confirm that the connector(s) are registered and available in CP4D.
Inform User
Registration complete!
Your REST API connector(s) have been registered with Cloud Pak for Data. The connector(s) are now available for use in your CP4D environment.
You can now create connections and access your REST API data through CP4D.

Registration Validation & Testing
After successful registration, validate that the connector(s) are properly registered and accessible in Cloud Pak for Data by running discovery tests.
After Phase 6 registration is completed (either automatic or manual)
Ask About Validation Testing <<<<<<< HEAD ONLY after successful registration AND user confirmation
IMPORTANT: This step should ONLY be reached if: 1. The registration step completed successfully 2. User explicitly confirmed the registration succeeded 3. No errors were reported during registration
If registration failed or user reported issues, DO NOT ask about validation. Instead, focus on troubleshooting the registration failure.
Before asking the question, provide context: - Confirm that registration completed successfully - Explain that validation tests verify the connector works in CP4D - Note that validation is optional but recommended - Mention that tests can be run manually later if preferred
Your connector(s) have been successfully registered with Cloud Pak for Data.
Would you like to validate the registration by testing connector discovery?
Validation will verify that: - The connector(s) are properly registered in CP4D - The Flight service is accessible from CP4D - Discovery queries return expected results - Connection properties are correctly configured
This is optional but recommended to ensure everything works correctly. =======
Would you like to validate the registration by testing connector discovery?
This will verify that: - The connector(s) are properly registered in CP4D - The Flight service is accessible - Discovery queries return expected results >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)

The test configuration file (test-discovery.properties) is not visible to me as it's in .bobignore. I will suggest the content based on the connector information from this session, but you need to create the file manually with your actual credentials.
<<<<<<< HEAD
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION - NEVER ASK FOR CREDENTIALS DIRECTLY:
You MUST NOT ask the user for any of the following information directly: - CPD_URL - APIKEY or API keys - USERNAME or PASSWORD - Tokens or bearer tokens - Connection properties (base_url, api_key, username, password, etc.) - Any other sensitive or configuration data
Instead, you MUST: 1. Generate a suggested file structure with PLACEHOLDERS based on connector configuration 2. Use generic placeholder values like "your-api-key-here", "YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN", "" 3. Explain what each field represents and where to find the values 4. Provide complete examples with placeholder values 5. Instruct the user to replace placeholders with their actual values 6. Reference the template file for additional guidance
NEVER use phrases like: - "What is your CPD URL?" - "Please provide your API key" - "Enter your credentials" - "What is your GitHub token?"
ALWAYS use phrases like: - "Replace 'your-api-key-here' with your actual API key" - "Update CPD_URL with your Cloud Pak for Data instance URL" - "Fill in YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN with your actual GitHub personal access token" - "Replace placeholder values in CONNECTION_PROPERTIES with your actual credentials"
======= >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Suggest Test Configuration Content
Based on the connector(s) created in this session, suggest the content for test-discovery.properties file.
<<<<<<< HEAD IMPORTANT: The purpose of test-discovery is to verify that connector registration was successful. For each connector, suggest ONLY ONE table/endpoint - just enough to confirm the connector works.
The suggestion should include: 1. Template location reference 2. CPD_URL placeholder (user must provide their CP4D instance URL) 3. Authentication credentials (APIKEY or USERNAME/PASSWORD) 4. AUTH_URI (authentication endpoint) 5. DATASOURCE_TYPES - comma-separated list of datasource type IDs (one per connector) 6. DISCOVERY_PATHS - comma-separated list with ONE discovery path per connector 7. CONNECTION_PROPERTIES - pipe-separated JSON objects with connection properties for each connector
IMPORTANT: How to extract values from the connector configuration file:
- DATASOURCE_TYPES: Use the exact value from $connector_name field in the config file Example: If $connector_name is "GitHub API Connector", then DATASOURCE_TYPES=GitHub API Connector
- DISCOVERY_PATHS: Select ANY ONE key from $tables object and prefix it with forward slash Example: If $tables has "REPOSITORIES", "ISSUES", "PULL_REQUESTS" - choose just ONE: /REPOSITORIES For multiple connectors, select one table from each: /REPOSITORIES,/PROJECTS
For each connector, extract: - Datasource type ID: exact value from $connector_name field (do NOT convert to lowercase or add underscores) - Discovery path: ANY ONE key from $tables object, prefixed with forward slash (typically the first one) ======= The suggestion should include: 1. Template location reference 2. CPD_URL placeholder (user must provide their CP4D instance URL) 3. CPD_TOKEN placeholder (user must provide their bearer token) 4. DATASOURCE_TYPES - comma-separated list of datasource type IDs from the connector descriptors 5. DISCOVERY_PATHS - comma-separated list of discovery paths (first endpoint path from each connector) 6. CONNECTION_PROPERTIES - pipe-separated JSON objects with connection properties for each connector
For each connector, extract: - Datasource type ID from the connector descriptor ($connector_name field, converted to lowercase with underscores) - First endpoint path from the $tables configuration >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules) - Authentication type and required properties from $authentication field - Base URL from $hostname field
Based on your connector configuration, here's the suggested content for test-discovery.properties:
```properties <<<<<<< HEAD # Copy from connectors-forge/scripts/test-discovery.properties.template # and fill in your actual values
# CPD Configuration CPD_URL=https://your-cpd-instance.cloud.ibm.com
# Authentication (choose ONE method): # Option 1: IBM Cloud API Key APIKEY=your-api-key-here # Option 2: Username/Password (for on-premises CP4D) #USERNAME=your-username #PASSWORD=your-password
# Authentication endpoint # IBM Cloud: https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token # On-premises: https://your-cpd-host/icp4d-api/v1/authorize AUTH_URI=https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token
# Connector Configuration (based on your connectors) # DATASOURCE_TYPES: Use exact $connector_name value(s) from config file # DISCOVERY_PATHS: Select ONE table per connector (for registration verification only) DATASOURCE_TYPES=GitHub API Connector DISCOVERY_PATHS=/REPOSITORIES CONNECTION_PROPERTIES={"base_url":"https://api.github.com","api_key":"YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN"} ```
Example with multiple connectors (one table per connector): ```properties DATASOURCE_TYPES=GitHub API Connector,Jira REST API DISCOVERY_PATHS=/REPOSITORIES,/PROJECTS CONNECTION_PROPERTIES={"base_url":"https://api.github.com","api_key":"TOKEN1"}|{"base_url":"https://jira.example.com","username":"user","password":"pass"} ```
Note: We test only ONE table per connector to verify registration was successful. If you need to test all tables, you can run discovery tests separately for each table later.
Replace: - CPD_URL with your Cloud Pak for Data instance URL - APIKEY with your IBM Cloud API key (or use USERNAME/PASSWORD for on-premises) - AUTH_URI with the correct authentication endpoint for your deployment - YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN and other credentials in CONNECTION_PROPERTIES with actual values
Note: The scripts will automatically obtain a bearer token using your credentials, so you no longer need to provide a pre-acquired token.
1. Create a file named test-discovery.properties in the connectors-forge/scripts/ directory
2. Use the template at connectors-forge/scripts/test-discovery.properties.template as a reference
3. Copy the suggested configuration above and replace placeholders with your actual values: - CPD_URL: Your Cloud Pak for Data instance URL - Authentication: Choose either APIKEY (IBM Cloud) OR USERNAME+PASSWORD (on-premises) - AUTH_URI: Authentication endpoint (update for on-premises if needed) - API keys, passwords, and other credentials in CONNECTION_PROPERTIES
4. Ensure the file is saved in the correct location: connectors-forge/scripts/test-discovery.properties
Note: The test scripts will automatically obtain a bearer token from your credentials, so you don't need to manually acquire a token beforehand. ======= # Copy from connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.properties.template # and fill in your actual values
# CPD Configuration (YOU MUST PROVIDE THESE) CPD_URL=https://your-cpd-instance.cloud.ibm.com CPD_TOKEN=your-bearer-token-here
# Connector Configuration (based on your connectors) DATASOURCE_TYPES=connector1_id,connector2_id DISCOVERY_PATHS=/endpoint1,/endpoint2 CONNECTION_PROPERTIES={"base_url":"https://api1.example.com","api_key":"YOUR_KEY"}|{"base_url":"https://api2.example.com"} ```
Replace: - CPD_URL with your Cloud Pak for Data instance URL - CPD_TOKEN with your authentication token - YOUR_KEY and other credentials with actual values
1. Create a file named test-discovery.properties in the connectors-forge/codeless/ directory
2. Use the template at connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.properties.template as a reference
3. Copy the suggested configuration above and replace placeholders with your actual values: - CPD_URL: Your Cloud Pak for Data instance URL - CPD_TOKEN: Your bearer token (get from CP4D UI or ibmcloud CLI) - API keys, passwords, and other credentials in CONNECTION_PROPERTIES
4. Ensure the file is saved in the correct location: connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.properties >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Have you created the test-discovery.properties file with your configuration?
Execute Validation Tests User confirms test configuration file is created
<<<<<<< HEAD
CRITICAL: Test execution requires careful monitoring and user confirmation. - Execute the test script and wait for completion - Ask user to confirm the test results (success/failure) - Do NOT assume success - wait for explicit confirmation - If tests fail, provide troubleshooting guidance - Only show success messages if user confirms tests passed
======= >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Run Discovery Test Script
Execute Test Script <<<<<<< HEAD connectors-forge/scripts/test-discovery.sh connectors-forge\scripts\test-discovery.ps1 ======= connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.sh connectors-forge\codeless\test-discovery.ps1 >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
Runs the OS-specific test script that: - Reads configuration from test-discovery.properties - Calls CP4D Connection Service API for each connector - Tests discovery without creating saved connections - Reports success/failure for each connector - Counts discovered assets
The script selection is based on the operating system: - Unix/Linux/macOS: test-discovery.sh (requires execute permissions: chmod +x) - Windows: test-discovery.ps1 (PowerShell script)
Monitor Test Results
Monitor the script output for: - HTTP status codes (200-299 = success) - Number of assets discovered per connector - Any error messages or failures
Analyze Results
The script will provide: - Summary of successful vs failed tests - Detailed results for each connector - Asset counts for successful discoveries - Error messages for failures
All connectors return HTTP 200-299 status codes Discovery returns expected assets for each connector No authentication or connection errors
Review error messages from script output Verify CPD_URL and CPD_TOKEN are correct Check that DATASOURCE_TYPES match registered connector IDs Verify CONNECTION_PROPERTIES have correct credentials Ensure Flight service is deployed and accessible Check network connectivity to CP4D and Flight service Offer to re-run tests after fixes
Interpret Test Results
<<<<<<< HEAD
CRITICAL: Result interpretation must be based on actual test output. - Ask user to confirm what the test results show - Only display success_message if user confirms all tests passed - Display partial_success_message if user reports mixed results - Display failure_message if user reports all tests failed - Provide appropriate troubleshooting based on actual results - Do NOT assume or guess the test outcome
What were the results of the validation tests? 1. All tests passed successfully 2. Some tests passed, some failed 3. All tests failed 4. I need help interpreting the results
User confirms all tests pass =======
All tests pass >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
✓ Registration validation successful!
All connector(s) are properly registered and accessible in Cloud Pak for Data. Discovery tests completed successfully with assets returned for each connector.
Your connectors are ready to use in CP4D.
Some tests pass, some fail
⚠ Partial validation success
Some connectors passed validation while others failed. Review the detailed results above to identify issues with specific connectors.
Successful connectors are ready to use. Failed connectors need troubleshooting before they can be used.
All tests fail
✗ Registration validation failed
None of the connectors passed validation tests. This typically indicates: - Registration did not complete successfully - Flight service is not accessible - Configuration errors in test-discovery.properties - Network connectivity issues
Please review the error messages and troubleshooting tips above.
Manual Validation Instructions User prefers to run validation manually
To manually validate your connector registration:
1. Create test-discovery.properties file: <<<<<<< HEAD - Location: connectors-forge/scripts/test-discovery.properties - Template: connectors-forge/scripts/test-discovery.properties.template ======= - Location: connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.properties - Template: connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.properties.template >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules) - Fill in your CPD_URL, CPD_TOKEN, and connector configurations
2. Run the test script:
For Unix/Linux/macOS: <<<<<<< HEAD chmod +x connectors-forge/scripts/test-discovery.sh connectors-forge/scripts/test-discovery.sh
For Windows: .\connectors-forge\scripts\test-discovery.ps1 ======= chmod +x connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.sh connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.sh
For Windows: .\connectors-forge\codeless\test-discovery.ps1 >>>>>>> 5b83da7 (change deployment to ssh and fix bob mode rules)
3. Review the test results: - Green checkmarks (✓) indicate successful discovery - Red X marks (✗) indicate failures - The script will show asset counts and error messages
4. Troubleshoot any failures using the tips provided by the script
Let me know the results or if you need help interpreting them.
Workflow Complete
After successful deployment, optional registration, and optional validation, provide summary and next steps.
Number of connectors created
Deployment type used (Codeless or Developer)
Deployment option used (Local, Self-Hosted, etc.)
Registration status (Registered or Skipped)
Service endpoint URL (if applicable)
How to access and test the connector(s)
How to stop/manage the deployment
Test the connector(s) by making API calls through the Flight service If registered with CP4D: Create connections in CP4D UI Monitor logs for any issues Configure client applications to use the connector endpoint
Always ask about additional connectors after completing each configuration Present all four deployment options clearly, indicating placeholders For implemented options (Local, Self-Hosted), offer automatic or manual execution For placeholder options (Serverless, OpenShift), explain future functionality Execute Gradle tasks sequentially, waiting for each to complete All Gradle tasks must be run from the sdk-gen directory Provide clear error messages and recovery options Confirm successful deployment before completing
Multiple connectors can be created and deployed together in a single container. All DSL files in the resources directory will be included.
Local deployment requires Docker to be installed and running on the user's machine.
Self-hosted deployment requires proper server credentials and network access.
Serverless and OpenShift deployments are placeholders for future implementation.
The deployment script (deploy-container.sh) must have execute permissions.
